FastAPI dependency injection: How would: Use FastAPI's Depends() to inject an auth dependency that validates a JWT/OAuth2 token and returns the current user, a database session dependency that yields a session and closes it after the request via a generator with try/finally, and shared validation dependencies (e.g. pagination params) reused across endpoints. Chain dependencies (a dependency can itself depend on others) to build layered checks like auth -> tenant scoping -> permission check without repeating logic per route.
FastAPI database design: How would you m: Use SQLAlchemy (or an async ORM) with a connection pool sized to the expected concurrency and Cloud SQL connection limits, manage schema changes with Alembic migrations run in CI/CD before deploy, and wrap multi-statement operations in explicit transactions with rollback on error. Add retry logic for transient connection errors (e.g. Cloud SQL Auth Proxy reconnects) but never retry a transaction that already partially committed side effects outside the DB.
FastAPI background work: When would you: Use BackgroundTasks only for short, best-effort work that can be lost on process restart (like sending a non-critical notification), since it runs in-process after the response is sent with no durability. For anything that must survive a crash or needs retries/scheduling - emails, report generation, downstream sync - use Pub/Sub or Cloud Tasks to hand off to a separate worker, which decouples request latency from the actual work and gives you durable retry semantics.
FastAPI testing: How would you test endp: Use FastAPI's TestClient (or httpx.AsyncClient for async tests) to call endpoints in-process without a real server, and override dependencies with app.dependency_overrides to inject fake auth or a test database session per test. Use a transactional test database (rollback after each test) or an in-memory/test container database for isolation, and write explicit tests for error paths - invalid input, missing auth, not-found - not just the happy path.
FastAPI rate limiting: How would you imp: Implement a dependency or middleware that checks a token bucket or sliding-window counter keyed by client ID/API key, backed by Redis or Memorystore so limits are enforced consistently across multiple service replicas. Return 429 with a Retry-After header when the limit is exceeded, and expose per-client quota usage via a metrics endpoint so teams can see how close they are to their limit before it becomes an incident.
FastAPI error handling: How would you de: Define a consistent error response schema (error code, message, optional field-level details) using a Pydantic model, and register custom exception handlers with @app.exception_handler() for domain exceptions and validation errors so every error - expected or unexpected - returns the same shape. Avoid leaking stack traces or internal details to clients in production; log the full exception server-side and return a sanitized message with a correlation ID clients can report.
FastAPI versioning: How would you versio: Version via URL prefix (/v1/, /v2/) or a header-based scheme, mounting versioned routers so old and new versions can run side by side. Introduce breaking changes only in a new version, keep the old version live with a documented deprecation timeline and a Deprecation/Sunset response header, and monitor usage metrics on the old version to know when it is safe to remove.
FastAPI caching: How would you add cachi: Cache expensive or frequently-read responses in Redis/Memorystore keyed by a deterministic cache key (endpoint + normalized query params), with a TTL appropriate to how stale the data can be. Invalidate explicitly on writes that affect the cached data (delete or update the specific key) rather than relying only on TTL expiry, and consider cache stampede protection (e.g. a short lock or jittered TTLs) for high-traffic keys.
FastAPI WebSockets: When would you use W: Use WebSockets when you need low-latency, bidirectional communication (chat, live collaboration, streaming LLM tokens); use SSE for simple one-way server-to-client streaming, which is easier to load balance and works over plain HTTP. WebSockets keep long-lived stateful connections, which complicates horizontal scaling (need sticky sessions or a pub/sub backplane like Redis to fan out messages across replicas) and requires explicit handling of reconnects, backpressure, and idle timeouts.
FastAPI multi-tenant design: How would y: Resolve the tenant early via a dependency (from the auth token, subdomain, or header) and thread it through every downstream query so data access is always scoped by tenant ID, ideally enforced at the database layer (row-level security or a tenant_id filter baked into the ORM query builder) rather than trusted per-endpoint. Track per-tenant usage against quotas in a shared store, and add tests that specifically verify one tenant cannot read or mutate another tenant's data.
FastAPI API gateway: How would you deplo: Deploy FastAPI on Cloud Run or GKE behind a global external HTTPS load balancer (or API Gateway/Apigee for policy enforcement), terminate TLS at the load balancer with a managed certificate, and forward auth via a validated JWT or IAP so FastAPI trusts identity from a header rather than re-implementing auth. Propagate a trace/request ID from the gateway into FastAPI middleware so requests can be correlated end to end in Cloud Trace or OpenTelemetry.
FastAPI contract-first design: How would: Define request/response models with Pydantic so FastAPI auto-generates an accurate OpenAPI schema from the code, or, for true contract-first workflows, author the OpenAPI spec separately and generate the Pydantic models/route stubs from it so client and server never drift. Publish the generated schema in CI, diff it against the previous version to catch accidental breaking changes, and auto-generate client SDKs from the schema so consumers do not hand-write brittle HTTP calls.
